title: podman.service fails when run by the display manager
body:
### Issue Description

`contrib/systemd/user/podman.service.in` starts podman whenever a user session starts.  But lightdm is implemented as a user session for a user with limited permissions (I assume other display managers are the same, but haven't checked).

When I log out or switch user, a lightdm session starts and its `podman.service` immediately exits with status 125.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. use a Linux desktop distribution
2. ensure no systemd session is running for user `lightdm`
  * e.g. `kill` the relevant `systemd --user` process
3. log out or switch user (exact steps depend on your desktop)
4. wait a few moments for the login manager to start
5. observe the systemd logs
  * e.g. do ctrl+alt+F1 or log back in again and scroll up
  * note: `sudo journalctl -ru podman.service` only shows the system service - you can find the issue by doing `sudo journalctl -r` then searching for `podman.service`

### Describe the results you received

Systemd logs contain:

```
podman.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=125/n/a
podman.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
```

### Describe the results you expected

Logs do not contain an error.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

Suggested fix:

1. change `contrib/systemd/user/podman.service.in` from a symlink to a copy of `contrib/systemd/system/podman.service.in`
2. add a line like:

```diff
 StartLimitIntervalSec=0
+ConditionUser=!@system
 
```

This would disable the service for all system users, not just lightdm.  It also requires `ConditionUser=`, which was added to systemd in 2017.  Finally, breaking the symlink would increase your maintenance burden.

Happy to resubmit this as a PR if you're OK with that solution, or to leave you to make a cleverer fix :)

comments.body:
Why is the podman.service is enabled? The service should not be enabled by default so podman should not run by default with system user as such I do not see why we should change the service file.

Second changing the service file to not run for system users might break some users who successfully use the service as system user so I don't see that as valid fix.


If the debian packages enables the service by default I suggest you file a bug against debian as it should not be doing that IMO.

Looks like it's started by default because it's `WantedBy=default.target`.  Rather than asking distro's to work around that problem, how about changing the line to `WantedBy=multi-user.target`?  That would start it slightly sooner on graphical systems, and not start it by default for users.

Are there any other services that should not be enabled by default when run in user sessions?

> WantedBy=multi-user.target

That target does not exists in the user session so it cannot be used ever in that context.

IMO all podman services should not be enabled by default.

Ok, so just to confirm - if I spend today finding a target that actually works as discussed, and come back with a PR that does `s/WantedBy=default.target/WantedBy=<whatever>.target` for all files in `contrib/systemd/*/*.service.in`, that PR would be likely to be accepted?

Well you can try to find such target then we can have a look, but I did spend a fair amount of time on these systemd problems and the user seesion really only has default.target if you want something to actually start on login.

IMO the issue here is that someone (your distro in this case) chooses to enable the unit as default. That is wrong regardless of what target is used. Podman does not need the service to run. It works totally fine without it as such that is the wrong default.
A user may wish to manually enable it with systemctl enable which is fine and needs a proper working target

Hmm, how about adding a `system/podman.target.in` that's `WantedBy=default.target`, and a `user/podman.target.in` that isn't?  Then everything works as expected by default, and if I want to start all the services on login, I can override my `podman.target` to be `WantedBy=default.target` without messing around with other services or inconveniencing other users on my system.

I don't see what this changes, why make things more complicated than they need to?

> I can override my podman.target to be WantedBy=default.target

Well that means a user must know how to do that over the current straight forward `systemctl enable` command.

Why try to work around the root cause? The services should not be enabled as default and the user should simply opt in via `systemctl enable podman.socket`. Then if there are users that cannot run podman it simply will not run unless someone enables/starts the service for this user.

Oh!  Sorry, I completely missed that `podman.service` was bound to the socket.  I'll get to that in a moment...

I do hear the point about getting distributions to disable services by default.  I'll be happy to talk to Debian if it comes to it, but I expect they'll say "WantedBy=default.target means you want this to start by default, so it's not our bug".  Also, a solution here upstream will work for other distro's too.  So I'm pushing for solutions in podman itself, but failing that I just need enough detail to overcome the obvious objections.

In the case of `podman.service`, why not just delete the `[Install]` section altogether?  [Poettering's original Socket Activation tutorial](https://0pointer.net/blog/projects/socket-activation.html) shows socket-activated services don't need an `[Install]` section, and a quick check on my system doesn't show any complaints.  Users who want podman to start sooner can always opt in.

That would still mean e.g. `podman-restart.service` starts when users log in.  But that's not necessarily related to this issue, and to be honest I don't see the problem with it.

Yes in theory we could remove the install section from the service if it is socket activated, but it is there now and remove it might break backwards compat, also see https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/25232

> That would still mean e.g. podman-restart.service starts when users log in. But that's not necessarily related to this issue, and to be honest I don't see the problem with it.

I don't know what your error with the service running under lightdm is but I would assume any podman command would face the same issue as the podman service as they do the same thing under the hood.

The restart unit is best effort really and we strongly recommend using quadlet to manage proper systemd units for the container so that the containers are tracked properly in systemd.
But besides that the main issue with running any podman command by default is that it "locks" in the podman database paths/storage driver, which means if you try to change some storage.conf setting to change some setting later it will can cause problems. And if the user never run podman before themselves they will end up totally confused why things are not working. (yes this )

>  I expect they'll say "WantedBy=default.target means you want this to start by default, so it's not our bug"

I doubt that, please just file the bug link the issue here. I have worked with Reinhard before and I can contact him directly if needed but this really should just go through proper distro bug channel.

There is no way for us upstream to specify if a systemd unit should be enabled by default or not (at least in the systemd unit AFAIK). The WantedBy line is not an indicator for that at all, it just specifies the unit/target when it should trigger but a user still must enable (i.e. create the right symlink for systemd to do that). Just because the line is in the unit does not mean it should be enabled as default, it only gives users the option to use the `systemctl enable` command.

In order to specify the actual distro defaults there are "preset" files to manage it. And if you look at fedora package for example 

>     Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/podman.socket; disabled; preset: disabled)

The preset is disabled and fedora packaging is mostly what upstream recommends as we (upstream maintainers) also maintain the fedora package.

Fair enough, I'll contact Debian and get back to you.  Just to make sure, can you confirm the argument is:

- `podman.socket` _should_ start system-wide at boot and when users log in
- `podman.service` _should not_ be enabled by default, either for user sessions or for the system session
  - this behaviour was never intended, and regressions are unlikely because it's started as required
- `podman-clean-transient.service` and `podman-restart.service` _should not_ be enabled by default, either for user sessions or for the system session
  - this behaviour was never intended, and users should be encouraged to consider more robust alternatives anyway

Also, here are some side issues that sprang from this conversation but don't affect the main point...

> I don't see what this changes, why make things more complicated than they need to?

Ignoring `podman.service` for a moment, let's say I'm fine with best effort, and I just want the podman utilities to work when I log in.  I'd be a happier user if I had a `podman.target` (or some better name like `podman-maintenance.target` or something), because a future version of podman can just add more services to the target, without me having to keep track of everything.  Not related to this bug, but worth mentioning for reference.

> I don't know what your error with the service running under lightdm is but I would assume any podman command would face the same issue as the podman service as they do the same thing under the hood.

Is there anything I can do to illuminate the problem?  It might be as simple as e.g. lightdm not having access to some directory.

> The restart unit is best effort really and we strongly recommend using quadlet to manage proper systemd units for the container so that the containers are tracked properly in systemd.

Could you add a comment to that effect in `podman-restart.service.in`?  Or in `man podman-start`?  That's useful information and those are the only places I would think to look.

> podman.socket should start system-wide at boot and when users log in

I have no strong opinion on the socket as it does no harm, podman will only be executed once a client connects. But as reference it is not enabled by default in fedora so I would not say it *should* but it can be.

> podman.service should not be enabled by default, either for user sessions or for the system session
 > this behaviour was never intended, and regressions are unlikely because it's started as required

If the podman.socket is used yes. 


> podman-clean-transient.service and podman-restart.service should not be enabled by default, either for user sessions or for the system session 

yes podman-clean-transient.service specifically is only needed for a very specific use case (transient store) which is never the default and 99.9% of user or more will never use it. 
podman-restart.service is only needed when a container is created with `--restart always` so the container starts afater boot again. However we strongly recommend using quadlet `podman-systemd.unit(5)` instead to manage container with systemd as this tracks the cgroups correctly and prevents restart issues, i.e. when conmon crashes. Sure that part can be documented better.

Then there is podman-auto-update.service/.timer which should also not be enabled by default, IMO users should explicitly opt in. 


I would argue we (upstream) should remove the [Install] section from podman.service and podman-auto-update.service as they are triggered by the other unit. However I have some backwards compat concerns here so I like to defer that until a podman v6 release.

--- 

> Ignoring podman.service for a moment, let's say I'm fine with best effort, and I just want the podman utilities to work when I log in. I'd be a happier user if I had a podman.target (or some better name like podman-maintenance.target or something), because a future version of podman can just add more services to the target, without me having to keep track of everything. Not related to this bug, but worth mentioning for reference.

That assumes you actually need them, I would argue most users don't so why run extra services/commands that do not do anything useful. I mean yes we can consider some kind of target to hook all our units into it but I personally think people should opt into the parts they actually need. Maintaining extra unit files puts extra burden on us maintainers to keep it updates/tested, etc... 


--- 




> > I don't know what your error with the service running under lightdm is but I would assume any podman command would face the same issue as the podman service as they do the same thing under the hood.
> 
> Is there anything I can do to illuminate the problem? It might be as simple as e.g. lightdm not having access to some directory.

Well show the proper error message that was printed. But yeah I don't really care about that part, if this is a locked down user I am sure there are plenty of things that break podman and I am not interested in debugging that.

Thanks.  [Bug filed](https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=1098808).

> > Is there anything I can do to illuminate the problem? It might be as simple as e.g. lightdm not having access to some directory.
> 
> Well show the proper error message that was printed. But yeah I don't really care about that part, if this is a locked down user I am sure there are plenty of things that break podman and I am not interested in debugging that.

Here's the log, plus [`sudo journalctl SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER=podman --since='Feb 24 15:24:09' -o json-pretty`](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18945568/podman.log) ('Feb 24 15:24:09' is when the service started).

```sh
$ sudo journalctl SYSLOG_IDENTIFIER=podman --since='Feb 24 15:24:09'
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245233]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="/usr/bin/podman filtering at log level info"

Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245233]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=error msg="cannot find UID/GID for user lightdm: no subuid ranges found for user \"lightdm\" in /etc/subuid - check rootless mode in man pages."
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245233]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=warning msg="Using rootless single mapping into the namespace. This might break some images. Check /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid for adding sub*ids if not using a network user"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245232]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=error msg="cannot find UID/GID for user lightdm: no subuid ranges found for user \"lightdm\" in /etc/subuid - check rootless mode in man pages."
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245232]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=warning msg="Using rootless single mapping into the namespace. This might break some images. Check /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid for adding sub*ids if not using a network user"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245234]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="/usr/bin/podman filtering at log level info"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245231]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=error msg="cannot find UID/GID for user lightdm: no subuid ranges found for user \"lightdm\" in /etc/subuid - check rootless mode in man pages."
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245231]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=warning msg="Using rootless single mapping into the namespace. This might break some images. Check /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid for adding sub*ids if not using a network user"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245234]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="Using sqlite as database backend"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245234]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=error msg="cannot find UID/GID for user lightdm: no subuid ranges found for user \"lightdm\" in /etc/subuid - check rootless mode in man pages."
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245234]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=warning msg="Using rootless single mapping into the namespace. This might break some images. Check /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid for adding sub*ids if not using a network user"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245284]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="/usr/bin/podman filtering at log level info"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245284]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="Using sqlite as database backend"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245337]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="/usr/bin/podman filtering at log level info"
Feb 24 15:24:09 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245337]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:09Z" level=info msg="Using sqlite as database backend"
Feb 24 15:24:17 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245296]: 2025-02-24 15:24:17.868726241 +0000 GMT m=+8.239254203 system refresh
Feb 24 15:24:17 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245337]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:17Z" level=info msg="Setting parallel job count to 13"
Feb 24 15:24:17 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245337]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:17Z" level=info msg="Using systemd socket activation to determine API endpoint"
Feb 24 15:24:17 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245337]: Error: unexpected fd received from systemd: cannot listen on it
Feb 24 15:24:17 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245337]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:17Z" level=info msg="Received shutdown.Stop(), terminating!" PID=245337
Feb 24 15:24:17 andrews-2024-laptop podman[245284]: time="2025-02-24T15:24:17Z" level=info msg="Setting parallel job count to 13"
```

The only thing that jumps out is `Error: unexpected fd received from systemd: cannot listen on it`, which seems to be from [service_abi.go line 49](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/1e7f810f714240f5d68f92baa1ab39ee53a249f5/cmd/podman/system/service_abi.go#L49).  I don't really understand what that message is trying to say, or if it's even relevant.  Can it be bumped up to priority 4 and changed to something clearer?

> unexpected fd received from systemd: cannot listen on it: cannot listen on it

That means whatever triggered the unit did cause systemd to set the LISTEN_PID env var to register that we are socket activated but then did not provide a valid socket fd, i.e. udp socket or not fd at all.
We need a unix socket or tcp (something that we can actually listen on)

And you are right that error does only matter to podman.service specifically, another podman should work based on that.
